content.full_text:
RAvI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES
COMMISSION
May 30,
1995
Hope Kingma-Rymek
Western Ecological Services Company,
Inc.
Environmental Planning and Resource Management
La Plaza,
4340 Redwood Highway, Building
B
San Rafael, California
94903
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—95—141
Dear Ms.
Kingina-Rymek:
You are writing to request advice regarding your obligations
under the revolving door provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”)
1
QUESTION
Does Section 87406, the Milton Marks Revolving Door
provision, prohibit you from assisting in writing restoration
plans for your present employer on a project which was a previous
case study while you interned with the California Coastal
Commission?
-
--
-
-
CONCLUSION
You are not prohibited from assisting your employer in the
writing of restoration plans to be submitted to the California
Coastal Commission.
You may not,
however,
make oral or written
communications before the California Coastal Commission for a
period of
12 months after leaving your position with the
California Coastal Commission.
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—95—141
Page
2
FACTS
You were employed as a part—time Environmental Services
intern with the California Coastal Commission
(“CCC”)
between
March 29,
1994 and February 17,
1995.
You were hired by Western
Ecological Services Company, Inc.,
(“WESCO”)
on February 21,
1995.
A concern over possible conflict of interest has been raised
in relation to the Big Lagoon restoration project,
a project on
which WESCO is collaborating with Phillip Williams and Associates.
Your responsibilities while working for the CCC included
assisting the wetlands coordinator in writing a draft procedural
guidance document entitled Procedural Guidance For Evaluating The
Performance Of Wetland Mitigation And Restoration Prolects In
California’s Coastal Zone.
This document is intended to provide a
framework for the review of wetland mitigation plans and wetland
mitigation projects.
However, this guidance document provides no
new regulations and cannot be cited,
quoted,
or relied upon as the
basis for recommendations or findings contained in any staff
report.
As part of this document, three case studies were completed
in order to evaluate the utility of the procedures for evaluating
wetland performance.
For these case studies,
you reviewed past
CCC permitted projects in the coastal zone that included a
wetlands restoration or mitigation component:
Lone Tree Slide/
Bolinas Lagoon in Mann County; Old Salinas River Channel in
Monterey County;
and Sweetwater Marsh Complex in San Diego County.
Each case study included a review of the existing permit file and
one or more site visits.
You indicated in a telephone
conversation that each of these projects was already completed at
the time of your case studies.
The Lone Tree Slide/Bolinas Lagoon project,
of which Big
Lagoon is an aspect,
is the basis of your concern regarding
possible conflict of interest.
The Big Lagoon restoration project
was in the initial planning stages while you were employed by the
CCC.
You stated that your work at the
CCC did not have any direct
connection with the permitting process for this project.
WESCO is currently seeking to participate in the planning and
writing of the restoration plan for Big Lagoon,
in cooperation
with Phillip Williams and Associates.
Your specific role at
WESCO, providing there is no conflict, would be to assist in
writing sections of the restoration plan.
You stated you would
not participate in presenting any of the information developed by
WESCO to
CCC staff or the Commissioners through the required
coastal development permit process.


File No.
A—95—141
Page
3
ANALYSIS
Milton Marks Postgovernmental Employment Restrictions Act
Section 87406 provides that no designated employee or member
of a state administrative agency shall, for compensation, act as
agent or attorney for,
or otherwise represent,
any other person,
by making any formal or informal appearance,
or by making any oral
or written communication, before any state administrative agency,
or officer or employee thereof, for which he or she worked or
represented during the 12 months before leaving employment, if the
appearance or communication is made for the purpose of influencing
administrative action,
or influencing any action or proceeding
involving the issuance,
amendment, awarding,
or revocation of a
permit,
license, grant,
or contract,
or the sale or purchase of
goods or property.
(Emphasis added.)
You indicated in a telephone conversation that you were a
designated employee with the CCC.
Accordingly, under Section
87406,
you may not, for a period of one—year after leaving office,
make any oral or written communication before the CCC.
However,
using your expertise to assist in writing sections of the
restoration plan on behalf of your employer would not be a
prohibited “appearance” or “communication” under Section 87406,
so
long as you are not identified in connection with your employer’s
efforts to influence the issuance,
amendment, awarding,
or
revocation of a permit,
by the CCC.
Please note, however, that
these activities may be restricted under the traditional revolving
door provisions discussed below.
Traditional Revolving Door Provision
Even prior to the enactment of the new ethics provisions of
the Act
the Act did providea life-time restriction on
-
postgovernmental employment activities under certain
circumstances.
Sections 87401 and 87402 provide:
No former state administrative official, after
the termination of his or her employment or term of
office, shall for compensation act as agent or
attorney for,
or otherwise represent,
any other
person (other than the State of California) before
any court or state administrative agency or any
officer or employee thereof by making any formal or
informal appearance,
or by making any oral or
written communication with the intent to influence,
in connection with any judicial, quasi—judicial or
other proceeding if both of the following apply:
(a)
The State of California is a party or
has a direct and substantial interest.


File No.
A—95—141
Page
4
(b)
The proceeding is one in which the
former state administrative official
participated.
(Section 87401.)
No former state athuinistrative official, after
the termination of his or her employment or term of
office shall for compensation aid, advise, counsel,
consult or assist in representing any other person
(except the State of California)
in any proceeding
in which the official would be prohibited from
appearing under Section 87401.2/
(Section 87402.)
Pursuant to Sections 87401 and 87402,
you may not appear
before or conuuunicate with the CCC, or any subdivision thereof,
with the intent to influence a ludicial, cruasi-ludicial or other
proceeding
3 I where the proceeding is one in which you
participated; and, the state is a party or has a direct and
substantial interest.
In addition,
Section 87402 prohibits you from aiding,
advising,
counseling, consulting or assisting any other person in
the proceeding.
For purposes of Sections 87401 and-87402,
you are considered
to have “participated” in a proceeding during your tenure at CCC
if you took part in the proceeding “personally and substantially
through decision,
approval, disapproval,
formal written
recommendation,
rendering advice on a substantial basis,
2/
Section 87400(b) defines a “state administrative official” as
every member, officer,
employee or consultant of a state
administrative agency who as part of his or her official
responsibilities engages in any judicial, quasi—judicial or other
proceeding in other than a purely clerical, secretarial or
ministerial capacity.
Your duties consisted of preparing
guidelines which may be used by applicants in submitting wetland
mitigation plans.
Thus you were involved in more than purely a
clerical, secretarial or ministerial capacity and qualify as a
state administrative official.
1’
“Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding” is defined in
Section 87400(c).
It means any proceeding, application, request
for a ruling or other determination, contract,
claim, controversy,
investigation,
charge, accusation, arrest or other particular
matter involving a specific party or parties in any court or state
administrative agency,
including but not limited to any proceeding
governed by Chapter 5
(commencing with Section 11500)
of Division
3 of Title
2 of the Government Code.


File No.
A—95—141
Page5
investigation or use of confidential information.”
(Section
87400(d).)
In your letter you stated that after the completion of the
draft guidelines, three case studies were completed in order to
evaluate the procedures outlined in the draft guidelines for
evaluating wetland performance.
For the case studies,
you
reviewed “past”
CCC permitted projects that included a wetlands
restoration or mitigation component.
The three projects included
the Lone Tree Slide/Bolinas Lagoon in Mann County;
Old Salinas
River Channel in Monterey County;
and Sweetwater Marsh Complex in
San Diego County.
The Big Lagoon restoration project was an
aspect of the Lone Tree Slide/Bolinas Lagoon project.
You stated
that this project was in the initial planning stages while you
were employed with CCC, but that your work did not have any
connection with the permitting process for this project.
Since you merely used the already completed Lone Star Slide/
Bolinas Lagoon project as a case study to test your guidelines and
neither the results of your case study nor guidelines were
utilized in the approval or disapproval in the Big Lagoon
permitting process,
you did not “participate” in a proceeding as
used in Section 87402.
Thus,
you are not prohibited from aiding,
advising,
counseling, consulting or assisting any other person in
this proceeding or prohibited from using your expertise to write
sections of the Big Lagoon Restoration plan.
If you need to discuss this further,
or need to clarify an
issue, please feel free to call me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
- General Cr””
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
SGC/JET/ ak

however,
make oral or written
communications before the California Coastal Commission for a
period of
12 months after leaving your position with the
California Coastal Commission.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.

(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—95—141

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does Section 87406, the Milton Marks Revolving Door
provision, prohibit you from assisting in writing restoration
plans for your present employer on a project which was a previous
case study while you interned with the California Coastal
Commission?
-
--
-
-

CONCLUSION: You are not prohibited from assisting your employer in the
writing of restoration plans to be submitted to the California
Coastal Commission.
You may not,
however,
make oral or written
communications before the California Coastal Commission for a
period of
12 months after leaving your position with the
California Coastal Commission.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.

(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—95—141
